content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 22 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic) | **Chapter:** Deuteronomy 22 (30 verses) | **LC:** pp. 219–220 | **Vaticanus:** pp. 156–157

---

**1** You shall not see your brother's ox or his sheep going astray and ignore them. You shall bring them back to your brother. **2** And if your brother is not near you, or you do not know him, you shall bring the animal into your house, and it shall remain with you until your brother seeks it. Then you shall return it to him. **3** And you shall do the same with his donkey; you shall do the same with his garment; and you shall do the same with any lost thing of your brother's which he loses and you find. You may not ignore it.

**4** You shall not see your brother's donkey or his ox fallen down on the road and ignore them. You shall help him to lift them up.

**5** A woman shall not wear a man's garment, nor shall a man put on a woman's garment, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD your God.

**6** If you happen upon a bird's nest along the road, in any tree or on the ground, with young ones or eggs, and the mother sitting on the young or on the eggs, you shall not take the mother with the young. **7** You shall surely send the mother away, but the young you may take for yourself, that it may go well with you and that you may live long.

**8** When you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, so that you do not bring blood-guilt on your house if anyone falls from it.

**9** You shall not sow your vineyard with two kinds of seed, lest the whole yield be forfeited—the crop that you have sown and the yield of the vineyard. **10** You shall not plow with an ox and a donkey together. **11** You shall not wear cloth of wool and linen mixed together.

**12** You shall make tassels on the four corners of your garment with which you cover yourself.

**13** If a man takes a wife and goes in to her and hates her, **14** and brings charges against her and gives her a bad name, saying: "I took this woman, and when I came near her I did not find in her the signs of virginity"— **15** then the father of the young woman and her mother shall take and bring out the evidence of the young woman's virginity to the elders of the city at the gate. **16** And the father of the young woman shall say to the elders: "I gave my daughter to this man to be his wife, and he hates her; **17** and behold, he has brought a charge of shameful conduct against her, saying: 'I did not find in your daughter the signs of virginity.' But here are the signs of my daughter's virginity." And they shall spread the garment before the elders of the city. **18** Then the elders of that city shall take the man and discipline him. **19** And they shall fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the father of the young woman, because he has given a bad name to a virgin of Israel. And she shall be his wife; he may not divorce her all his days.

**20** But if this charge is true—the signs of virginity were not found in the young woman— **21** then they shall bring out the young woman to the entrance of her father's house, and the men of her city shall stone her to death with stones, because she has done a disgraceful thing in Israel by practicing sexual immorality in her father's house. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**22** If a man is found lying with a woman who is the wife of another man, both of them shall die—the man who lay with the woman and the woman. So you shall purge the evil from Israel.

**23** If there is a young woman who is a virgin betrothed to a man, and a man finds her in the city and lies with her— **24** then you shall bring both of them out to the gate of that city and stone them to death with stones—the young woman because she did not cry out in the city, and the man because he violated his neighbor's wife. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**25** But if the man finds the betrothed young woman in the open field, and the man seizes her and lies with her, then only the man who lay with her shall die. **26** But you shall do nothing to the young woman; she has committed no sin worthy of death. For this case is like that of a man who rises against his neighbor and murders him, **27** because he found her in the open field. Though the betrothed young woman cried out, there was no one to save her.

**28** If a man finds a young woman who is a virgin and is not betrothed, and seizes her and lies with her, and they are found— **29** then the man who lay with her shall give to the father of the young woman fifty shekels of silver, and she shall be his wife, because he violated her. He may not divorce her all his days.

**30** A man shall not take his father's wife, and shall not uncover his father's skirt.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Chapter overview.** Deuteronomy 22 moves from community obligations (vv. 1–4), through purity laws (vv. 5–12), to a lengthy section on sexual and marital law (vv. 13–30). The three agents broadly agree on the first section; significant divergences appear in vv. 5, 21, 25–28, and 30.

**v.1–4 "ignore" (LOGOS) vs. "hide yourself" (EMET) vs. "ignore" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *hithalalemta* = Hithpael of *alam*, "to hide oneself from." EMET renders the literal reflexive idiom; LOGOS/PNEUMA both render the practical meaning: "ignore." Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**v.5 "man's garment" (LOGOS) vs. "apparel of a man" (EMET) vs. "men's clothing" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *keli gever* = "implement/equipment of a man." EMET preserves the broader term *keli* ("apparel" is a slight concession). LOGOS: "garment." PNEUMA: "clothing." The parallel *simlat ishah* ("garment of a woman") confirms clothing is the primary referent. Consensus follows LOGOS.

**v.9 "forfeited to the sanctuary" (PNEUMA) vs. "become holy" (literal) vs. "forfeited" (LOGOS).** Hebrew *tiqdash* = "become holy/set apart." LOGOS: "forfeited." PNEUMA adds "to the sanctuary" (interpretive but accurate). Consensus follows LOGOS ("be forfeited") as the clearest rendering of the practical outcome without adding "to the sanctuary" beyond MT.

**v.21 "practicing prostitution" (LOGOS) vs. "sleeping around" (PNEUMA) vs. "commit prostitution" (EMET).** Hebrew *liznot* (Qal infinitive of *zanah*). All three differ. "Practicing prostitution" (LOGOS) renders *zanah* literally. PNEUMA's "sleeping around" is too colloquial. Consensus follows LOGOS but adjusts to "practicing sexual immorality" — *zanah* in this context denotes pre-marital intercourse rather than commercial prostitution, and "sexual immorality" better fits the context while preserving moral gravity.

**vv. 25–27 Rape vocabulary.** LOGOS: "seizes her." PNEUMA: "rapes her." EMET: "seizes her." The Hebrew *chazaq* + *shakhav* in v.25 and *taphas* + *shakhav* in v.28 both denote forced sexual intercourse. Consensus uses "seizes" in the body text (EMET/LOGOS) because the text itself immediately clarifies the coercive nature (she is not guilty), preserving the Hebrew vocabulary. PNEUMA's "rapes" in v.25 is an accurate interpretation but goes beyond the word *chazaq* alone.

**v.30 "uncover his father's skirt" vs. "dishonor his father's bed."** EMET/LOGOS: "shall not uncover his father's skirt" (literal euphemism, *lo yegaleh kenaf aviv*). PNEUMA: "must not dishonor his father's bed" (interpretive paraphrase). Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: the idiom is used consistently in Scripture (Ruth 3:9; Ezek 16:8) and should be preserved.

**Vaticanus note.** Vaticanus pp. 156–157 aligns with MT throughout. LXX v.17 uses *porne/porneuon* (prostitute/one who acts as a prostitute) rather than a cultic term, supporting the plain sexual reading of *zanah*.

---

*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
